summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Disclosure – Where the wife sought that the husband disclose two medical reports that were prepared for the purposes of proceedings before the Supreme Court of New South Wales – Where the husband opposed the application and sought that if the court determines that any likely diminished life expectancy of the husband is likely to assume probative relevance in these proceedings, then a single expert geriatrician be appointed – Where the wife did not accept the offer of the appointment of a single expert geriatrician – Where the parties are each approximately 78 years of age and were married for over fifty years – Where the property pool is contended to be approximately $200 million – Where the husband contends that no adjustment is sought in his favour in relation to matters of health – Where the wife contends that no adjustment is sought in the husband’s favour in relation to matters of health – Where the health of the husband is not a matter in issue and the medical reports are not relevant to an issue in the proceedings – Application dismissed.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

By Application in a Proceeding filed 24 December 2025, the applicant wife seeks orders that the first respondent husband disclose two medical reports that were prepared for the purposes of proceedings before the Supreme Court of New South Wales.
In support of her application, the wife relies upon an affidavit of herself and written submissions of her senior and junior counsel.
The husband, through his litigation guardian, relied upon an Amended Response, an affidavit of his solicitor and a Case Outline document prepared by his senior counsel. The Amended Response sought the following orders:
The Application in a Proceeding filed 24 December 2025 be dismissed.
In the event that contrary to the case of the husband, the court determines that any likely diminished life expectancy of the husband is likely to assume probative relevance in these proceedings, and only in that event, then a single expert geriatrician be appointed to be jointly instructed to opine on that issue and for that purpose the wife shall select one of the three experts proposed by the husband in the letter from McCabes dated 16 January 2026 who shall the be the appointed expert.
Each party pay their own costs of this application.
Any such order that the court deems fit.
These reasons explain why the wife’s application will be dismissed.

BACKGROUND

The parties are engaged in proceedings pursuant to Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) which are listed for final hearing commencing on 27 July 2026.
The parties are each approximately 78 years of age and were married for over fifty years. They broadly agree that the pool of assets is somewhere in the order of approximately $200 million. Joined to the proceedings are 16 other respondents which include their adult children.
The wife in her affidavit says that because of concerns as to the mental capacity of the husband, she commenced proceedings in the Supreme Court of New South Wales in May 2025 to determine, “whether the respondent husband has mental capacity” (wife’s affidavit, paragraph 3).
The wife says that in May 2025, His Honour made various orders for the husband to be examined by two medical practitioners. In September 2025, His Honour made further orders in relation to the medical reports produced following the examinations noting that the reports recorded that the husband was presently unable to manage his affairs (Notation 3 of Orders of September 2025 made by His Honour).
Her affidavit goes on to record the following:
Given the lack of capacity of the Respondent Husband, and the relevance of his health to his future care and future needs in these proceedings, I have sought disclosure of the Reports from him and his solicitors, and have not received a response...
7.1 email dated 27 October 2025 and time stamped 2:42pm,

7.2 email dated 4 November 2025 and time stamped 2:25am, and

7.3 email dated 14 November 2025 and time stamped 6:32am.
Upon receipt of the Reports, I wish to obtain expert opinion as to the Respondent Husband's future needs, and life expectancy.

(Reference to annexures omitted)
Presumably in response to the assertions of the wife in paragraph 7 as to the issues of the future needs and life expectancy, the husband’s solicitor swore an affidavit which relevantly records as follows:
On 16 January 2026, I wrote to Mr Amirbeaggi proposing three geriatricians to prepare a report on the life expectancy of [Ms Wojcik] and [Mr Wojcik]...
I proposed that a geriatrician prepare a report on the spousal parties’ life expectancy because:
20.1. I do not consider that [Mr Wojcik’s] state of health is a relevant factor under s 79 given the size of the matrimonial asset pool;

20.2. I am instructed that [Mr Wojcik] does not seek an adjustment in his favour based on his present state of health such that the Medical Reports prepared for the Supreme Court of New South Wales have no relevance to the issues in dispute in these proceedings; and

20.3. the Medical Reports prepared for the Supreme Court of New South Wales do not consider [Mr Wojcik’s] (or the parties’) life expectancies.

(Reference to annexures omitted)

The offer contained in that correspondence was not accepted by the wife.
The wife, through her senior counsel, submitted that consistent with the husband’s duty of disclosure as set out in the Act and the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) he has an obligation to disclose all information and documents relevant to an issue in the proceedings. In her senior counsel’s written submissions, it was submitted that an issue in the proceedings was the state of health of the husband and his mental capacity.
The written submissions include the following:
If the Court is satisfied that it is “on the cards” the reports will be relevant in these proceedings, then it is appropriate in the interests of the administration of justice that not only the First Respondent and the Respondents just listed are able to consider the reports, but that the Applicant is too. Indeed for her to be excluded from reviewing relevant material in the hands of the other parties would be inappropriate.
...
It is reasonable to predict that the reports contain assessments of the respondent’s state of health and his mental capacity, both of which may be relevant considerations for the Court under section 79(5), not simply as to the quantum of any property settlement, but the form it will take. In any event section 79(5)(v) requires the Court to have regard to any (relevant) fact or circumstance which in the opinion of the court, the justice and equity of the case requires be taken into consideration. It can thus hardly be ruled out that the contents of the reports may be relevant in the property adjustment proceedings; it is certainly “on the cards” they will be, and so the duty of disclosure applies to the reports.
The oral submissions of the wife’s senior counsel expanded upon the written submissions to contend that the husband’s capacity to manage a company informed what orders the Court might make bearing upon which assets each party might retain. By way of example, it was submitted that it would inform the making of the wife’s proposed Order 24 where she sought the transfer to her of the husband’s shares in a private company. The submission, as I understand it, was that in the event there were an issue as to the husband’s capacity, the Court would more readily transfer the husband’s shares to the wife as the husband was incapable of managing the company.
Senior counsel for the wife acknowledged that the husband did not seek an adjustment under s 79(5) of the Act in relation to his health and conceded that his client did not propose there should be any adjustment in the husband’s favour.
The husband’s senior and junior counsel’s written submissions included the following:
...The Court should refuse the application as the orders sought are not appropriate in the interests of the administration of justice because:
(a) the disclosure sought is not relevant to an issue in dispute and its disclosure will not have an impact on the outcome of the proceedings in circumstances where the matrimonial asset pool is in the hundreds of millions of dollars and, consistent with the size of the asset pool, the Husband does not seek an adjustment in his favour under s 79(5)(b) of the Act on account of the state of his health or the costs of his future care; and
...
The reports prepared by Dr [UU] and Professor [VV] have no relevance to an issue in the proceedings in circumstances where the matrimonial asset pool following a marriage of nearly fifty-two years is in the hundreds of millions of dollars. Given the size of the pool and the length of the marriage any order made under s 79 will provide the Husband with more than ample assets to meet any medical or caring expenses that he has or may have in the future. Consistent with this, the Husband is not seeking any adjustment in his favour under s 79(5)(b) on account of the state of his health. Moreover, even if the state of the Husband's health was nevertheless somehow still thought to be relevant, the reports prepared by Dr [UU] and Professor [VV] do not for example consider his current or likely medical and caring needs or their cost but are limited to whether the Husband "is a person who is incapable of managing his own affairs" which offers no insights as to the making of an appropriate property settlement. For effectively the same reasons, the reports prepared by Dr [UU] and Professor [VV] have no relative importance to the consideration of s 79(5)(b) as a factors and the disclosure or non-disclosure of the medical reports will have no effect on the outcome on the proceedings.

...
The Court has been reticent in the application of s 79 of the Act to take life expectancy into account unless it is a significant factor and supported by clear findings: Lawrie & Lawrie [1981] FamCA 73; (1981) FLC 91-102; Fontana & Fontana (2016) 55 Fam LR 410 at [26]; Dixon & Elliot [2020] Fam CA 1005.
The Husband does not contend that the question of the comparative life expectancies of the spousal parties is relevant - both parties are 78 years of age and there is no evidence, or even a suggestion, that the Husband's diminished capacity bears on the question of his life expectancy.
However, if contrary to the Husband's primary position, the Court considered the Husband's state of health and life expectancy to be relevant, the Husband in his Amended Response filed 23 January 2026 seeks the appointment of a geriatrician as a single expert to determine his life expectancy. This arises because in paragraph 8 of the wife's affidavit sworn 18 December 2025 she infers that the reports she seeks access to are relevant to the Husband's life expectancy and states her intention to seek a report as to that issue. The Husband contends that having regard to the quantum of the matrimonial estate and the ages of both parties that issue could not have probative relevance. However, in in [sic] the event that the Court is of a contrary view, then he proposed an alternative order to appoint a single expert.
The husband, through his senior counsel, tendered a copy of the orders made by His Honour in late 2025 which included a declaration pursuant to s 41(1)(a) of the NSW Trustee and Guardian Act 2009 (NSW) that the husband is incapable of managing his affairs.
Senior counsel for the wife in oral submissions in reply conceded that because of the orders and declarations made by His Honour in late 2025, there could be no factual issue as to the husband’s capacity but there remained an issue as to his life expectancy.
The husband’s submissions also addressed “considerations of comity” and protected confidences as a basis for objection to the making of the orders. It is unnecessary to address those objections as I am not satisfied, on the case as articulated by the wife, the reports bear on any issue in the proceedings.

DISCUSSION

The wife in her affidavit says that upon receipt of the reports, she wants to obtain expert opinions as to the husband’s future needs and life expectancy. Thus, central to the determination of the wife’s application is whether there is an issue in the proceedings as to the husband’s future needs and life expectancy.
If they are issues in the proceedings, then the Act and Rules, all other things being equal, would compel their disclosure.
However, as the argument developed it became clear that the assertion of issue was an illusion. The declaration made by His Honour J in late 2025 settles the issue of capacity as conceded by the wife’s senior counsel.
As to future needs, the husband’s senior counsel reaffirmed that stated by the husband’s solicitors that no adjustment was sought in his favour under s 79(5) of the Act in relation to matters of health. The wife’s senior counsel conceded that the wife was not proposing an adjustment under s 79(5) of the Act in favour of the husband for future needs. Consequently, health is not a relevant s 79(5) issue.
The submission that the husband’s health or incapacity informed what orders would be made is fallacious. The wife’s Amended Initiating Application seeks amongst other orders for the husband to transfer to the wife shares held by the husband in one particular company. The wife’s senior counsel submitted that the issue of the husband’s capacity would inform why such an order should be made. I do not accept the logic of that proposition as it does not accord with other orders where the wife seeks to transfer to the husband shares held by her in other companies. The submission has no foundation considering the declarations made by His Honour.
In so far as the wife may have contended in her affidavit that life expectancy was an issue, her subsequent conduct was inconsistent with its existence as an issue. If there ever really was an issue as to life expectancy, which is difficult to accept given the quantum of the pool and the ages of the parties, it remains inadequately explained why the wife did not accede to the offer of the husband’s solicitor to engage a geriatrician to assess the husband’s life expectancy. The wife did not need the reports to accept the husband’s offer. The fact that the offer was not accepted, nor was the alternate relief as proposed by the husband in his Response, satisfies the Court that it is not an issue.

DISPOSITION

The wife’s Application in a Proceeding filed 24 December 2025 will be dismissed.
